title: CRA Principal Residence Exemption
metaDescription: Learn about CRA principal residence exemption rental income Canada rules, deadlines, and penalties to avoid costly mistakes and ensure compliance with tax laws.
content:
As a Canadian landlord with 1-5 properties, understanding the CRA principal residence exemption rental income Canada rules is crucial to avoid costly mistakes and ensure compliance with tax laws. The CRA principal residence exemption allows homeowners to avoid paying capital gains tax when selling their primary residence. However, when you rent out your property, the rules change, and you need to report rental income on your tax return.

## Introduction to CRA Principal Residence Exemption
The CRA principal residence exemption is a tax deduction that allows homeowners to reduce their taxable income when selling their primary residence. To qualify for the exemption, the property must be your primary residence, and you must have lived in it for at least one year. However, when you rent out your property, you need to report the rental income on your tax return, using the T776 form. For example, if you rent out your property in Toronto for $2,000 per month, you would report $24,000 in rental income on your tax return.

## Understanding CRA Principal Residence Exemption Rental Income Canada Rules
The CRA principal residence exemption rental income Canada rules can be complex, but essentially, you need to report all rental income on your tax return, including rent, utilities, and any other payments you receive from your tenants. You can also claim deductions for expenses related to the rental property, such as mortgage interest, property taxes, and maintenance costs. For instance, if you pay $10,000 in mortgage interest on your rental property in Vancouver, you can claim that as a deduction on your tax return. You can learn more about [CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords: The Complete Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/cra-compliant-bookkeeping-canadian-landlords-complete-guide) to ensure you are meeting all the requirements.

## Reporting Rental Income on T776 Form
When reporting rental income on the T776 form, you need to include all income related to the rental property, including rent, utilities, and any other payments you receive from your tenants. You can also claim deductions for expenses related to the rental property, such as mortgage interest, property taxes, and maintenance costs. For example, if you pay $5,000 in property taxes on your rental property in Calgary, you can claim that as a deduction on line 914 of the T776 form. RentalOps can help you track your income and expenses and ensure you are meeting all the CRA requirements.

## CRA Deadlines and Penalties for Rental Income
The CRA deadlines for reporting rental income are the same as the deadlines for filing your personal tax return, which is April 30th of each year. If you fail to report rental income or make mistakes on your tax return, you may face penalties, such as fines or interest on the amount you owe. For instance, if you fail to report $10,000 in rental income, you may face a penalty of $2,000, plus interest on the amount you owe. You can learn more about [Failing to Report Rental Income to CRA: A Costly Mistake for Canadian Landlords](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/failing-to-report-rental-income-to-cra-a-costly-mistake-for-canadian-landlords) to avoid making the same mistakes.

## Provincial Rules and Regulations for Rental Income
Provincial rules and regulations for rental income vary across Canada, but essentially, you need to comply with both federal and provincial tax laws. For example, in Ontario, you need to register for a provincial tax account if you have rental income from a property in the province. You can learn more about the specific rules and regulations in your province by visiting the CRA website or consulting with a tax professional. RentalOps can help you navigate the complex tax laws and ensure you are meeting all the requirements.

## Common Mistakes Made by Small Landlords
Small landlords often make mistakes when reporting rental income, such as failing to report all income, claiming incorrect deductions, or missing deadlines. For example, if you fail to report $5,000 in rental income, you may face a penalty of $1,000, plus interest on the amount you owe. Another common mistake is claiming deductions for personal expenses, such as mortgage payments on your primary residence. You can avoid these mistakes by keeping accurate records and using a tool like RentalOps to track your income and expenses.

## Key Takeaways
* Report all rental income on your tax return, including rent, utilities, and other payments
* Claim deductions for expenses related to the rental property, such as mortgage interest and property taxes
* Keep accurate records and use a tool like RentalOps to track your income and expenses
* Comply with both federal and provincial tax laws
* Avoid common mistakes, such as failing to report all income or claiming incorrect deductions

## Conclusion
Reporting rental income can be complex, but with the right tools and knowledge, you can ensure compliance with CRA rules and avoid costly mistakes.
